title: Exact distribution of the output of a deep-layered machine
abstract: Deep-layered machines, in which each node computes a Boolean function of all nodes below it, underpin deep learning and digital computation. Yet the statistics of their global output function remain poorly understood. We derive the exact finite-depth distribution of the output of a machine with width $k$ and depth $n$. The distribution depends only on the Hamming weight of the output, and as $n$ increases favors functions with low and high Hamming weights. But this bias peaks at a crossover depth proportional to $2^k$ before collapsing onto the constant functions true and false.
summary: This paper rigorously derives the exact probability distribution of the output function for a deep-layered Boolean machine. It shows that while initial layers create complex structure, increasing depth causes the system's output to rapidly lose information and collapse into a simple coin toss between true and false. This highlights a finite structural limit inherent in purely compositional computation.
main_problem: The primary goal is deriving the exact finite-depth probability distribution of the global output function of a deep-layered machine.
main_result: The output distribution exhibits a crossover behavior: initially biased towards low/high Hamming weights, it eventually collapses to an almost equal coin toss between the constant 'true' and 'false' outputs as depth increases.
method: The analysis uses linear recurrence relations derived from a transition matrix approach applied to the probability vector over the Hamming weight of the output function.
model_or_system: The model is a deep-layered machine where each node computes an arbitrary Boolean function based on all nodes in the preceding layer. The system is characterized by its width $k$ and depth $n$.
key_observables: The probability distribution vector $\mathbf{q}(n)$ over the Hamming weight $w$, and the probabilities of the constant functions (true/false).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes symmetry such that all local functions sharing the same Hamming weight contribute equally to the probability evolution.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the structure of the machines and show the evolution of true/false probabilities (orange curves) versus network depth $n$, confirming a crossover at $n_c \simeq 2k$.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the mathematical framework using transition matrices, analyzes the spectral properties to find dominant eigenvalues, derives the time evolution $\mathbf{q}(n) = \mathbf{A}^{n-1} \mathbf{q}(1)$, and then interprets the physical meaning of the resulting crossover behavior.
why_interesting: The study provides an exactly solvable null model demonstrating how complex, structured information processing (like deep learning) can exhibit predictable, finite-depth limitations governed by spectral properties, which has parallels in understanding decoherence or complexity limits in quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Deep-layered machines. In a network of k arguments (a, b, . . .), each node depends on all k of the arguments below it, each of which depends on the k arguments below it, and so on, for a total of n levels. The goal of this paper is to determine the probability distribution of the global output Boolean function F(a, b, . . .) given a random assignment of local Boolean functions to the fs at the light gray nodes.

FIG. 2: Critical network depth. When the network depth reaches nc ≃2k, the Hamming weight distribution q(n) has flattened out, but true and false have yet to dominate. At this crossover, the output distribution p(n) is maximally U-shaped in absolute terms. The orange curves show the probability that the output is true or false as a function of n and for various k; they approach 1 with n. The black curves show the standard deviation of the interior of q normalized by its maximum value, as a function of n and for various k. Lower standard deviation means q is flatter. The curves decrease with n to an asymptote that approaches 0 with k.

FIG. 3: Experiments confirm our predictions. We com- pare our prediction of the probability q(n) of the output func- tion (lines) with computer experiments (points), for various values of the number of arguments k and network depth n. The vertical axis is the probability of an output with a given Hamming weight w; outputs with the same w have the same probability. In all cases, our experiments agree with our theory exactly or, when sampling, to within statistical significance. A For k = 2, we enumerated all of the configurations up to network depth n = 5.
